Réalisation, Conceptualisation, Réception (A Precursor of Digital Humanities? The First Automated Analysis of an Ancient Economic Network (Gardin & Garelli, 1961). Implementation, Theorization, Reception)
reference: ARCS. Analyse de réseaux pour les sciences sociales, issn : 2610-2609, https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-01870945
affiliations: University of Bordeaux - Centre Émile Durkheim
abstract: French Abstract: Dès les années 1950, les travaux de J.‐C. Gardin concernèrent à la fois l'archéologie et l'automatisation naissante du calcul numérique et de la documentation. En 1961, à partir de tablettes cunéiformes assyriennes documentant des relations économiques, il publia avec P. Garelli la première application automatisée de la théorie des graphes à des matériaux historiques. Elle fut ensuite largement ignorée tanten archéologie qu'en analyse de réseaux. Toutefois, depuis vingt ans, les revendications socio-épistémiques liées à la généralisation d'internet et de l'informatique (humanités numériques, archéologie computationnelle, etc.) ont accru l'intérêt porté aux travaux – jugés précurseurs – de Gardin. Fondé sur des archives et des publications, cet article défend la pertinence d'une sociologie historique du texte scientifique pour l'histoire de l'automatisation des sciences historiques. L'identification de Gardin comme précurseur influent d'une archéologie computationnelle est nuancée, en montrant que 1) malgré son accès facilité à des ressources (financières, instrumentales, etc.) alors rares et ayant pu favoriser la fondation d'une école ou d'une spécialité, il ne poursuivit pas cette ambition   2) les objectifs démonstratifs qu'il attribua à l'étude de 1961 du réseau économique ont varié entre les années 1960 (démontrer l'intérêt du calcul non numérique) et les années 1980 (légitimer la simulation en sciences sociales) mais n'ont, jamais, concerné « l'analyse de réseaux » – ou toute autre procédure algorithmique – en tant que telle. English Abstract: From as early as the 1950s, J.‐C. Gardin's work spanned both archaeology and the emerging automation of numerical computation and documentation. In 1961, with P. Garelli, he published the first automated application of graph theory to historical materials, working from Assyrian cuneiform tablets documenting economic relations. This work was then widely ignored both in archeology and network analysis. However, in the past twenty years, socio-epistemic claims related to the growth of the Internet and computing (digital humanities, computational archaeology, etc.) have brought a surge of interest in Gardin's work, which is now regarded as pioneering. Working from archive materials and publications, this paper shows how a historical sociology of scientific writings can be relevant to the history of automation in historical sciences. The paper examines Gardin's recognition as an influential forerunner of computational archeology, showing that : 1) although Gardin had access to resources (financial, instrumental, etc.) that were rare at the time, and could have provided material for the foundation of a school or a specialty, he did not however pursue this ambition   2) the demonstrative purposes pursued by Gardin with his study of 1961 economic networks varied between the 1960s (demonstrating the relevance of non-numerical computation) and the 1980s (legitimizing simulation in the social sciences), but were never concerned with network analysis as such.

titre: Integration of Transmedia in Teaching Ancient Greece Among Grade Nine Students Towards Effective Mainstreaming of Learning
reference: Proceedings Journal of Education, Psychology and Social Science Research, Vol. 4, Issue 1, 2017
abstract: This study determined the integration of transmedia in teaching Ancient Greece towards effective mainstreaming of learning in grade nine students in a national high school.  It made use of quasi-experimental research design with pre-test and post-test using purposive sampling and used mean to summarize the results. It used t-test of independent means to test the difference in the pre-test and post-test performances of the two groups, and t-test of correlated means to test the difference in the pre-test and post test performances of the two groups.  It made the following conclusions (1) There is no significant difference between the pre-test performance of students using and not using transmedia. (2) There is a significant difference between the pre-test and post-test performance of students using transmedia. (3) There is a significant difference between the pre-test and post-test performance of students not using transmedia. (4) There is a significant difference between the post-test performance of students using and not using transmedia.  The findings show that the use of technology specifically transmedia in teaching Ancient Greece is more beneficial to students as compared to the traditional method of teaching.

titre: Adam Smith (and Keynes, the Ancient Hebrew Israelite Prophets, Christ, Buddha, Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, Mohammed, Aquinas, Augustine, Abbess Ng Mui, Confucius, Ramakrishna, and Gandhi ) Against Jeremy Bentham (and the Modern Economics Profession)
abstract: Adam Smith (and Keynes, the Ancient Hebrew Israelite Prophets, Christ, Buddha, Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, Mohammed, Aquinas, Augustine, Abbess Ng Mui, Confucius, Ramakrishna, and Gandhi) were all practitioners of some form of Virtue Ethics. Jeremy Bentham (and the economics profession since 1790) and his followers are all practitioners of some form of Utilitarianism.The fundamental conflict turns on the misunderstanding of the concept of self interest. The Virtue ethics view of self interest is that it is one of the major virtues. Self interest is based on the virtue of Prudence for practitioners of Virtue Ethics. Smith liked to use the words “Self-Love”, as well as prudence and self interest, because, if you can’t love (care for) yourself, then you will never be able to love (care for) someone else.Prudence thus requires careful, judicious planning, hard work, circumspect behavior, a stick-to-it, never give up mentality, motivation, frugality, parsimony, saving, dedication, carefulness, and putting safety first. It allows the prudent brewer, baker, butcher, beautician, brick layer, and blacksmith to support themselves, their families and relatives while building up a surplus or nest egg as a safeguard against bad times or economic downturns. The virtue of prudence is a necessary condition that must be applied first. It puts one in a position to be able to practice the other virtues later if they can avoid the problem of corruption.Bentham’s definition of self interest is totally different from Smith’s. Self interest is defined as maximizing only one’s own utility, where money can be considered as proxy for Utility. Thus, for Bentham, it is the accumulation of more and more money (wealth) over time that is the beginning goal of one’s life and the ending goal of one’s life. It is the only viable goal in life if one is rational. Any other goal is irrational.For Smith, profit maximizing behavior is prudent behavior because it allows the hard working brewer, baker, butcher, beautician, brick layer, and blacksmith to build up a surplus or nest egg. This can be used to cover future unexpected costs, such as health problems, as well as to practice benevolence (charity) to help others. However, utility maximizing behavior is rejected by Smith because there are a number of other concerns or goals, such as honesty, justice, equity, fairness, etc. that are as important as economic consequences. For Bentham, these other goals are all besides the point because only economic consequences can count for Bentham, where a good consequence is a positive monetary outcome for an individual and a bad consequence is a negative monetary outcome. The concept of a moral judgment, in terms of Good or Bad and Right or Wrong have no place in Bentham’s system. Only the monetary outcome of any action is evaluated as good or bad. Thus, the Good Samaritan’s helping the robbed and badly beaten traveler, who is a Jew, would be viewed by Bentham as being reckless, irresponsible, hasty, emotional, dangerous, foolhardy, unwise and costly behavior, since the Good Samaritan will have to spend additional silver coins on his return trip home in order to cover the additional cost of the full recovery of the traveler that will serve to drain his bag of silver coins. The virtue of courage displayed here (or practiced by, for instance, Socrates, Christ, and Ng Mui) would be regarded as foolhardy and reckless by Bentham. (See my paper on the Good Samaritan parable at SSRN).

titre: What Diplomacy in the Ancient near East Can Tell Us about Blockchain Technology
reference: Berg, C 2017. ‘What diplomacy in the ancient near east can tell us about blockchain technology’, Ledger, vol. 2, pp. 55-64.
affiliations: Royal Melbourne Institute of Technology (RMIT University)
abstract: A blockchain is an institutional technology—a protocol—that allows for economic coordination between agents separated by boundaries of possible mistrust. Blockchains are not the only technology in history to have these characteristics. The paper looks at the role of the diplomatic protocol at the very beginning of human civilisation in the ancient near east. These two protocols—diplomatic and blockchain—have significant similarities. They were created to address to similar economic problems using similar mechanisms: a permanent record of past dealings, public and ritualistic verification of transactions, and game-theoretic mechanisms of reciprocity. The development of the diplomatic protocol allowed for the creation of the first international community and facilitated patterns of peaceful trade and exchange. Some questions about a generalised ‘protocol economics’ are drawn.

titre: Ancient Theories of Value: As Viewed from Modern Economic Theory
abstract: Aristotle was among the first thinkers to appreciate the difference between the use value and exchange value of the object of an economic exchange.   He drew not simply economic but, more profoundly, ethical conclusions from this distinction.  In this he was followed by Aquinas who distinguished two different types of conducting economic exchange.  Today we can recognize in these forms the modern equivalents of market exchanges and relational exchanges which take place within organizations.  The distinction between use value and exchange value also reveals what is often most puzzling about the economic thought of both Aristotle and Aquinas and, by extension, that of the entire Judaic, Christian, and Islamist tradition, that of the prohibition of interest on lent money.

titre: Роль Монеты в Развитии Экономики Древней Греции VI-IV вв. до н.э. (The Role of Coins in the Development of the Economy of Ancient Greece VI-IV Centuries B.C.)
affiliations: Russian Academy of National Economy and Public Administration under the President of the Russian Federation (RANEPA) - Laboratory of Historical and Cultural Studies
abstract: Russian Abstract: Экономика  античных  государств  представляет  собой  сложное  и многообразное  явление.  Одной  из  её  существенных  составляющих  является денежное обращение,  которое  с определённого момента  формируется  обращением монет. В настоящей работе речь пойдет о начальных страницах истории денежного обращения античного мира. English Abstract: The economy of ancient states is a complex and diverse phenomenon. One of its essential components is monetary circulation, which from a certain moment is formed by circulation of coins. In this paper we will talk about the initial pages of the history of the money circulation of the ancient world.

titre: Empires of Inequality: Ancient China and Rome
abstract: This paper analyzes the dynamics of income and wealth inequality in two of the largest ancient empires, Han China and Rome. Pervasive structural similarities emerge from this comparative survey. In both cases, resource concentration at the top of society was greatly amplified by rent-seeking and predatory behavior that was commonly linked to privileged access to governmental functions and institutions. The exercise of or proximity to political power were crucial means of elite enrichment. Yet the same features also served to constrain the growth and persistence of large fortunes as the violent seizure and recirculation of elite wealth ensured ongoing redistribution within the ruling class and among its associates. In the long run, imperial stability was conducive to growing economic inequality.

titre: Prices and Markets in Ancient Rome
reference: MIT Department of Economics Working Paper No. 16-02
affiliations: Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) - Department of Economics
abstract: This paper argues that there was enough buying and selling (emptio venditio) in the early Roman Empire to show that there were markets, and there were enough markets at the time that there was a market economy. I use three examples to make these points, one from my research, one from the research of a younger ancient historian, and one from an older ancient historian. These examples also show the usefulness of a few economic techniques like regression analysis, comparative advantage, and assuming that farmers respond to market prices.

titre: A Fable about Sumptuous Accounting™ and other Sci-Fi Adventures on Parallearth
abstract: Sumptuous Accounting™ is an accounting methodology that incorporates the non-financial impacts of commercial transactions. These economic externalities – positive or negative – are not reflected in conventional accounting methodologies. They are often excluded from market prices and financial asset valuations. Such omissions can result in market failure if price sensitive decisions are disconnected from their broader societal consequences. The methodology employs a rigorous three-layer data architecture that maps onto and redresses known reasons for the divergence of non-financial data: (1) capturing raw, objective non-financial data throughout supply chains to fix measurement divergence  (2) formulating these data into sumpt values via standardised calculations to accommodate scope divergence  and (3) applying jurisdiction-specific, normalised scores that reflect policy priorities to facilitate weight divergence. Optionally, Sumptuous Accounting™ can be extended to calculate dynamic Value Added Tax (VAT) rates and to compensate for externalities. This can align market incentives with societal values, functioning as a behavioural tax mechanism that can both penalise perceived negative externalities and reward positive ones. The methodology for dynamic VAT is designed with procedural consistency, transparency, and non-discriminatory application in mind. This ensures compatibility with WTO and GATT rules by treating domestic and imported goods equivalently. The methodology offers a unified and auditable framework for internalising externalities across all economic activities, supporting better informed decision-making for consumers, businesses, and regulators.

titre: Technology, Accounting, and Research
reference: Accounting Horizons, Forthcoming Accounting Horizons, 2025[ 10.2308/HORIZONS-2025-173 ]
affiliations: University of Florida - Warrington College of Business
abstract: The article provides a primer on accounting from the vantage point of technology and is written for a broad audience beyond accounting academics. I make four points. First, accounting itself is a technology, and I use double-entry bookkeeping as an example. Second, accounting evolved as businesses adopted new technology and engaged in new types of transactions. Third, the tension between the two primary objectives of financial reporting—valuation and stewardship—has been an undercurrent in the development of generally accepted accounting principles in the U.S. Last, accelerated technological advances present an increasingly daunting challenge for accounting and exciting opportunities for accounting researchers.

titre: The Impact of Accounting Information Quality on the Cost of Equity Capital of Companies Considering the Role of Information Asymmetry and the Comparability of Financial Statements
affiliations: University of Texas at Arlington - Department of Accounting
abstract: The aim of this study is to explain the impact of accounting information quality on the cost of equity capital of companies and to examine the moderating role of information asymmetry as well as the simultaneous moderating effect of this variable and the comparability of financial statements in this context. The study period spans five years, from the beginning of 2013 to the end of 2017, and the research sample includes 91 companies listed on the Tehran Stock Exchange. Multivariate regression was used for statistical analyses. The results of the analyses indicate that accounting information quality has an inverse effect on the cost of equity capital, such that higher accounting information quality leads to a reduction in the cost of equity capital, and vice versa. Additionally, information asymmetry has a direct relationship with this effect. However, the simultaneous effect of financial statement comparability and information asymmetry on the impact of accounting information quality on the cost of equity capital was not confirmed. The results of this study in the first two hypotheses are consistent with prior research conducted in the United States examining the impact of accounting information quality on the cost of equity capital, but the result of the third hypothesis indicates a lack of alignment with those findings.

titre: A Quantitative Analysis of Greenwashing Practices in Corporate Carbon Accounting and Its Impact on Stakeholder Trust and Regulatory Compliance
abstract: This study examines the prevalence of greenwashing in corporate carbon accounting and the factors that influence these practices. This study examines the correlation between carbon disclosures (Scope 1, 2, and 3 emissions), regulatory oversight, stakeholder confidence, the utilization of carbon offsets, and sustainability performance about the propensity for greenwashing, as companies encounter mounting pressure to reveal their greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. A quantitative methodology is utilized, examining data from 100 firms through descriptive statistics, correlation analysis, and regression analysis to evaluate the hypotheses. The results indicate that firms with elevated Scope 1 emissions and increased regulatory oversight are more prone to engage in greenwashing. Nonetheless, stakeholder trust, symbolic reporting, and the utilization of carbon offsets do not substantially affect greenwashing behavior. The research underscores the significance of thorough carbon reporting, stringent regulatory structures, and the reduction of operational emissions to combat greenwashing. This research enhances the existing literature on corporate sustainability practices and provides practical recommendations for policymakers, businesses, and stakeholders to enhance transparency and accountability in carbon accounting.

titre: The Essentials of the Linear Regression Model: Comprehensive Summary with Application to Growth Accounting
abstract: This short presentation analysis covers the essentials of the linear regression estimation analysis in an easy-to-read form with an example application to Growth Accounting. The objective is to support the data user in accomplishing the statistical robustness or validity of estimated empirical results.

titre: The Behavioural Economics of Goodwill: How Market Sentiment Becomes Accounting Loss
abstract: This paper introduces the Trust Bankruptcy Ratio as a novel metric to quantify how behavioural sentiment and digital outrage catalyse goodwill impairments in modern corporations. Through a blend of accounting data, sentiment analysis, and case studies—including Air India, Maggi, Boeing, Volkswagen, Wells Fargo, and Meta—it bridges behavioural economics with tangible financial consequences. By integrating VADER sentiment scores, IFRS impairment models, and real-world scandals, it proposes that goodwill is not merely an intangible asset, but a societal call option susceptible to behavioural debt and market retribution.

titre: Accounting Information System in Digital Based Company Scope
abstract: The development of information technology has become an important factor in supporting the sustainability and growth of the business world in the digital era. The implementation of information systems, especially Accounting Information Systems (AIS), makes it easier for companies to manage data, accelerate transactions, and improve operational efficiency and decision-making quality. AIS plays a role in processing and presenting accurate financial information, thus supporting the achievement of the company's competitive advantage. Information technology also functions as a strategic tool in dealing with uncertain business dynamics, strengthening the company's position in the market, and encouraging the integration of business and technology strategies. With the implementation of the right information system and in line with the organization's vision and mission, companies can optimize the potential of information resources to improve overall financial and operational performance. Digital-based companies utilize the integration of information technology, process automation, and data analytics to improve efficiency, accuracy, and transparency in financial reporting. This article discusses the role and impact of implementing AIS in digital companies, covering aspects of data security, real-time reporting, and integration with other systems such as Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) and cloud computing

titre: Management Accounting Philosophy and the Challenge of Past Trends' Failures (A Perspective of Overoptimism)
abstract: Management accounting is mostly about costing and budgeting, and has been developed to be strategic management accounting to manage the cost and budgeting strategically. I present in this article my view regarding how far the philosophy of management accounting and strategic costs has added to the international economy and the international goods and services markets. Management accounting had a past vision in the 90s, affected by new trends back then, that could have created sustainability to help future generations. Nevertheless, complications and complexity hit the world in several aspects, and the direction of production and services has shifted to new technologies, financial innovation and artificial intelligence that require good management and cost accounting systems to account for them, and also can be used to develop the techniques of management accounting used for costing and budgeting and fairly compete in the markets. New challenges require expanding and developing the philosophy of management accounting, well known to accountants since its appearance in the context of accounting, but not replacing it. I present in this article how the world is looking like after all the past trends that seem to have failed due to past overoptimism.

titre: Scenes of Co-Parenting: A Counseling Session on the Future of Environmental Accounting Research
abstract: The world of academic research often mirrors the intricacies of personal relationships. Driven by contrasting ideologies, different schools of thought can clash even when united under the same banner. Such is the case with environmental accounting research, a field teetering between the realms of different paradigms. The tension between them—personified here as M, I, and C—threatens the very future of the “child” that they co-parent: environmental accounting research. In an attempt to reconcile their differences and find a path forward, they turn to a counseling session, hoping for resolution, or at the very least, a mutual understanding. What follows is a scene of conflict, compromise, and hopefully, clarity, where academic disputes take the form of familial quarrels. A co-parenting arrangement in crisis, but one that carries implications far beyond the personal.

titre: Forensic Accounting and Valuation in Delaware Litigation
reference: William & Mary Business Law Review, Vol. 16, pp. 577–621 (2025)  George Mason Legal Studies Research Paper No. LS No. 25-10
affiliations: George Mason University - Antonin Scalia Law School
abstract: This Article describes the forensic accounting and valuation tools available to Delaware litigators in commercial and corporate litigation as well as family law. Further, it details the licensing process and standards applicable to forensic accountants and shows how the methods utilized in this profession can assist litigators in a variety of contexts. Delaware cases in which courts have accepted or limited the use of expert forensic accountant testimony are explored and general principles for utilizing forensic accounting expertise in Delaware litigation are developed. The uses of forensic accounting in Delaware litigation are demonstrated across appraisal actions, divorce cases, fraud and fiduciary actions and investigations, and economic damages estimation.

titre: Linking Recursive Utility, Accounting Information, and Equity Valuation
affiliations: University of Southern Denmark (SDU Business School)
abstract: This paper introduces recursive utility functions to accounting-information based equity valuation. A new theoretical model together with a recipe for empirical implementation, is proposed. The new application of recursive utility in this paper, provides new understanding of accounting-information and equity valuation, e.g. the model embeds the C-CAPM, CAPM and the theoretically correct weighting mechanism of the two (i.e. no reason to horse race the two risk-adjustment models).

titre: The Role of Mental Accounting in Risk-Taking and Spending: A Metaanalysis of the House-Money Effect
abstract: This study systematically analyzes the house-money effect, a phenomenon in which people become more financially risk-taking and wasteful after receiving unexpected income. It aims to identify the general tendencies and factors that influence this effect, because the results reported in previous studies are inconsistent. A total of 57 continuous and 18 binary outcome studies were included in this meta-analysis. A random-effects model was used to pool the effect sizes, and a low-to-moderate house-money effect (g = 0.37, rr = 1.33) was confirmed. However, high heterogeneity was observed, and the strength of the house-money effect varied widely, depending on the situation. The subgroup and meta-regression analyses revealed several moderators. While a strong effect was observed in the controlled experimental environment, the effect was weakened when it was closer to a real-world environment. For continuous outcomes, the effect was particularly pronounced in students and Asian regions, and the effect size decreased as the publication year increased, suggesting the limited universality of the house-money effect. In the publication-bias analysis, a slight bias was detected using multiple methods. This suggests that the true effect size may be smaller, supporting the theory that the house-money effect is reproducible only under certain conditions.

titre: From mean-variance analysis to mental accounting and back: Bridging contributions of Markowitz to portfolio selection
affiliations: George Washington University - School of Business
abstract: Modern portfolio theory considers investors whose preferences are often represented by a function defined over the means and variances of portfolio returns and specified with a risk aversion coefficient  see Markowitz (1952a). In Das et al. (2010), however, Markowitz and three co-authors consider an investor who has: (i) mental accounts (hereafter, 'accounts') with different investing motives  (ii) preferences within an account that are specified by some maximum threshold probability of the account's return being less than or equal to some threshold return  and (iii) threshold probabilities and threshold returns (hereafter, 'thresholds') that possibly vary across accounts. Like the optimal portfolio in Markowitz's model, optimal portfolios within accounts in Das et al.'s model are on the mean-variance frontier and so is the corresponding aggregate portfolio if short selling is allowed. Bridging Markowitz's contributions, several analytical results are here uncovered. These results encompass: (1) the losses arising from misspecifying a risk aversion coefficient in Markowitz's model  (2) the impact of using thresholds instead of a risk aversion coefficient to specify investor preferences on portfolio selection  and (3) the risk aversion coefficients of investors in Markowitz's model who select the same portfolios as an investor in Das et al.'s model selects within accounts.

titre: Adaptation to Integration: A Review on the Indian Accounting Standards (IND AS) in Global Financial Reporting Convergence
affiliations: Maharshi Dayanand University - Institute of Management Studies & Research
abstract: The paper reviews the journey of IND AS adoption, its challenges, and its impact on Indian corporates and stakeholders. It explores the transformation in financial reportingpractices and how these standards have enhanced transparency, reduced information asymmetry and improved global investor confidence.

titre: Accounting for Income Tax Uncertainty and Corporate Tax Avoidance: International Evidence
reference: WU International Taxation Research Paper Series No. 2025-05
affiliations: Vienna University of Economics and Business
abstract: This study investigates whether the accounting for uncertain tax treatments (IFRIC 23) under a principle-based reporting standard affects corporate tax avoidance. IFRIC 23 provides guidance on recognition, measurement, and disclosure of uncertain tax treatments in financial statements. Under this interpretation, firms must assume that tax authorities have full knowledge of the tax information reported in the financial statements. Using a difference-in-differences design in OECD and EU countries, I compare IFRS firms with firms reporting under local standards. I find that IFRS firms reduce the gap between statutory and reported effective tax rates relative to non-IFRS firms. The magnitude corresponds to approximately $1.4 million in additional reported tax expense per firm-year for the median firm. The decline is concentrated among relatively compliant firms, whereas firms with high pre-adoption levels of tax avoidance exhibit limited responsiveness. Cross-country analyses further indicate that the association between IFRIC 23 adoption and tax avoidance varies with institutional enforcement intensity. Overall, these findings suggest that financial reporting guidance on tax uncertainty can influence reported tax outcomes, although its effectiveness depends on firm-level incentives and institutional environments.

titre: Accounting Information Usage and Trading by Retail Investors: Evidence from Integrated Trading Platform
abstract: This registered report investigates self-directed retail investors' information choices and trading decisions on an integrated trading platform that provides timely and convenient access to accounting information. The analyses reveal that these investors access a mosaic of information, with a high proportion not firm-specific. In accessing accounting disclosures, retail investors are more interested in media articles providing summaries or expert analyses than original filings. Retail trades on this integrated trading platform are more informed than another retail trading platform providing little information services while exhibiting no significant differences in informativeness compared with institutional trades using Bulge Bracket platforms. In particular, trade informativeness is more pronounced when there are accounting disclosures. The evidence suggests that self-directed retail investors can benefit from a trading environment that provides rich and convenient access to a mosaic of information, particularly timely accounting disclosures.

titre: Australia's Offense of "Intentional False Dealing with Accounting Documents"
affiliations: University of Colorado School of Law
abstract: The information contained herein is not, and should not be relied upon as, legal advice, and is not a substitute for qualified legal counsel.

titre: Corporate Carbon Accounting: Current Practices and Opportunities for Research
reference: TRR 266 Accounting for Transparency Working Paper Series No. 177
abstract: This article reviews current practices in corporate carbon accounting and highlights opportunities for future research. The common framework for determining and reporting corporate greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions today is the GHG Protocol. Like financial accounting standards, this framework includes overarching objectives, principles for conceptual guidance, and procedures for determining key outcome variables. Their design and implementation, however, often result in disclosures that obscure firms’ actual emissions and decarbonization progress. Recognizing the growing demand for transparency, standard-setters worldwide have recently introduced regulations for carbon accounting and reporting. These regulations require companies to disclose decision-useful information on their emissions. Yet, they have also largely adopted the GHG Protocol for how companies should determine and report their emissions. Accounting scholars now have the opportunity to develop solutions that will make corporate carbon accounting an effective tool in combating climate change.

titre: Merchants, Markets, and Trade Networks: A Historical Perspective on Accounting
reference: No
affiliations: London School of Economics & Political Science (LSE) - Department of Accounting and Finance
abstract: Ball (2024) has recently argued that double-entry bookkeeping/accounting ‘emerged around the same time and place as the manorial system began to collapse and transactions in goods and capital markets began to flourish in its stead’.  ‘So, no-one invented double entry accounting: ultimately, markets were responsible for its emergence.’ Here I argue that markets and related institutions of various forms can be seen to have evolved in the varying economic conditions of different civilisations throughout history. They are probably a necessary condition for the emergence of DEB but not a sufficient condition as it is not until 13 th and 14 th century Italy that its burgeoning markets are supported by DEB. In particular the Imperial Chinese experience shows how systems approximating DEB have emerged elsewhere.

titre: Integration of SAP Fund Management with Financial Accounting
abstract: SAP Fund Management (FM) and Financial Accounting (FI) integration allows organizations to circumvent many of the problems associated with disconnected budget and accounting systems, including high cost, oversight, and compliance risks. This integration creates a closed-loop system connecting elements like budget allocation, pre-allocation, real-time posting, and reporting. The capabilities of this integration include commitment accounting, automated funds availability control, dynamic budget management, and audit-ready compliance reporting.

titre: Carbon Shelters: Carbon Accounting as Tax Law
reference: 114 Calif. L. Rev. (forthcoming 2026) Vanderbilt Law Research Paper No. 5141694
abstract: This Article provides the first comprehensive account of the reconstruction of energy tax law that occurred under the Biden administration. In the past, federal energy policy offered carrots and sticks aimed selectively at specific sources of emissions (e.g. power plants) and specific green alternatives (e.g. solar and wind), even as academics urged the use of universal sticks like a carbon tax. But Congress has now charted a new path: universal carrots, or tax credits for any and all zero-emission energy technologies. The only way to implement universal carrots is to estimate the carbon intensity of every subsidy applicant. This is the task of carbon accounting. The Article makes two main arguments about the emergence of carbon accounting inside tax law. First, carbon accounting is surprisingly well-suited to tax law, because it will be informed by tax law’s experience with parallel normative and analytical principles, including a comprehensive tax base, additionality, liability shifting, and rate blending. But second, just as the income tax is susceptible to “tax shelters,” so too will firms develop “carbon shelters” that qualify for green subsidies while covertly making use of high-emission energy. Because of the difficulty of anticipating every carbon shelter in advance, an anti-shelter strategy needs deliberately over-broad anti-abuse rules, including some modeled on similar rules from tax law. If policymakers are to avoid inadvertently subsidizing unlimited emissions, they must be prepared to compromise on the principle of technology neutrality that motivates universal carrots in the first place.

titre: How Do Companies Manage Energy? A Systematic Literature Review on Energy Cost Accounting, Energy Efficiency, and Energy Management
reference: Applied Energy, volume 397, 2025[ 10.1016/j.apenergy.2025.126372 ]
abstract: It is imperative to recognize the indispensable role energy plays in sustaining business operations. Nevertheless, energy and topics associated with energy are often neglected, despite the crucial nature of information on energy consumption for the needs of managers in decision-making and the act of managing a company. As energy and its application are interdisciplinary topics, information is scattered throughout the literature. Consequently, the absence of a comprehensive study systematically collecting, describing and analyzing the current state of knowledge is striking. To address this deficiency, this systematic literature review synthesizes information from 39 papers published in 19 journals. The extracted contents of the papers were categorized, summarized, and analyzed according to their contribution toward first and second stage allocation of energy costs, energy measurement, energy efficiency, energy strategy, and energy-related investment decisions. The results of the study indicate a significant research gap in empirical investigations. The interdisciplinarity of the investigated topics leads to scattered information and frameworks for a structured analysis of corporate energy cost practices are lacking. To address this gap, this paper develops a framework for the collection of information on corporate energy practices based on the findings. This article puts forth the proposition that empirical investigations of corporate energy practices warrant scholarly attention. Such empirical studies need to find out what practice is doing, as the depth of the current state of literature is inadequate.

titre: Eco-Environmental Economic Accounting and its Policy Application in the Central Africa Sub-Region
affiliations: Regional Advisory Commision on Financial Markets
abstract: Eco-Environmental Economic Accounting and its Policy Application in the Central Africa sub-region gathers innovative and original contributions to economic structural adjustments in Africa. The authors are concerned with the impacts of economic reforms and policy recommendations that regional governments could propose. The book blends critical country-specific studies with theoretical modelling and technical considerations for case studies. Given the free market policy adopted by the Central African states, the perspective is that, for a long time, we might have to confront the new situation of Central African economies, which is very different from their wish for their development. This ocean of economic theories and practices ended up not saying, nor the measures of its economic performance, nor, and even less, taking into account the root concepts for long-term accounting to shape the integrated global economic framework needed and respond to changes and common global economic interests. In short, to the point where economists have no single understanding. We must be more ingenious and find our way out of a situation where accounting and decision-making suffer from methodological dystopia. The challenge is thus to ensure that our efforts positively impact the stakes of our modern problems and provide long-lasting elements for future reflections and studies on increasingly complex structures while keeping in mind the need to uphold a simple presentation to improve comprehension.

titre: Disciplinary Boundaries of Accounting: Structure and History of Research Programs and Institutions
abstract: Modern accounting, which arose as a result of institutionally determined forms of business organization at the very beginning of the second millennium AD, has now gone through several stages of development, turning from a simple technology for recording business data into a socio-economic institution, a system of rules and standards for business information support, administrative and managerial practice, profession and, at the same time, into an academic discipline. What institutions constitute and support accounting practices? What does the practice and economic discipline of “accounting” consist of? What exactly is accounting science? How can one determine that a particular research question or a particular problem is relevant to its subject field? Why do article authors and journal editors believe that a particular study, publication, or project meets the standards of our discipline? Is the knowledge that different authors presented in their articles scientific? Answers to all these questions require a special approach to defining the subject field of accounting and its disciplinary matrix. In addition to the picture of the subject field, it is equally important to understand how this state of affairs came to be. Studying the history of how accounting institutions arose, developed and changed in response to changes and needs in the development of the economy and society will allow us to better understand the relationship of accounting with other socio-economic institutions, practices and its capabilities in solving socio-economic problems. Russian economists, including those working in the field of accounting, often recognize accounting by default as a purely practical discipline that serves the organization’s relations with certain groups of interested parties (stakeholders), one of the applied sections of economic science, one of the financial disciplines. The idea of its subject field is, as a rule, quite narrow - it is interpreted mainly as a set of accounting methods, techniques, concepts, reporting forms  the relationship of accounting with other areas of knowledge, institutions, sciences and practices is rarely considered. Very few papers on accounting topics other than methodological ones are published in Russian, and this is fundamentally different from the situation in the English-language scientific literature. In global science, accounting is an actively developing and rather complex structured discipline, which emerged from the traditional boundaries of economic disciplines in the last third of the twentieth century and has long been recognized as a social science (Bernstein, 1965, p. 34  Hopwood, Miller, 1994). This is facilitated by universal quantification, ranking, decision-making based on “objective” criteria, increased transparency and accountability, which determine the functioning of modern technologically advanced societies and are the result of the penetration of accounting into all social practices, even non-economic ones, over the past 20-30 years. Accounting has long occupied a strong place in the programs of leading universities and academic classifiers of economic literature. However, there are some gaps in the accounting research domain. Understanding the general picture of the subject field of a particular discipline (such as accounting) contributes to the structuring of science as a whole, gives an idea of the place of accounting among the socio-economic sciences, of areas of the intersection with other sciences and of the possible contribution of accounting to the solution of interdisciplinary problems. These issues determine the relevance of this study from a theoretical point of view.

titre: Accounting for Debt Securities in the Age of Silicon Valley Bank
reference: FRB of Boston Supervisory Research & Analysis Unit Notes No. 2023-01
abstract: This note examines the mixed measurement accounting approach for debt securities under U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles. It considers potential options for addressing the concerns with the accounting for held-to-maturity debt securities following the failure of Silicon Valley Bank.

titre: Revenue Recognition of Islamic Financial Institutions: The Curious Choice of Applying IFRS 9 Over IFRS 15 in Accounting for Trade-Based Contracts
abstract: As opposed to conventional interest-based lending, Islamic financial institutions (IFIs) are guided by the Shariah principles that emphasize trade, asset backed transactions and risk sharing. Instead, contrary to IFRS 9 (Financial instruments), Islamic contracts i.e. Sales-based, Equity-based, Rental-based contracts are only treated as mere financial instruments under current accounting standards, which are inappropriate for Islamic financial transactions. Such a misalignment has implications for the transparency of financial reporting, the accuracy of reporting and Shariah compliance of such financial reporting. The primary purpose of this study is to analyze the appropriateness of IFRS 9 in Islamic finance, and to provide an argument that IFRS 15 (Revenue from Contracts with Customers) is the most suitable standard for revenue recognition in IFIs. This work highlights a number of important points: (1) IFRS 9 classifies Islamic contracts as Murabaha, Ijarah, and Sukuk as financial assets, not trade transactions causing financial statements to not conform to the real meaning of Islamic contracts  (2) The Expected Credit Loss (ECL) model as defined by IFRS 9 as transferring risk when Islamic finance is commonly known as risk-sharing  (3) Revenue generated in Islamic contracts are from selling or leasing assets, not accruing interest and thus IFRS 15 is more appropriate. (5) IFIs face regulatory pressure to adopt IFRS 9 which conflicts with their trade-based institutional identity  (6) IFIs applying IFRS 9 makes them resemble conventional banks through complex transactions around conventional loan practices that damages their credibility as Islamic financial institutions. (7) By adopting IFRS 15, IFIs would achieve better transparency and fair revenue recognition while reinforcing compliance with Shariah principles. In conclusion, Islamic financial institutions should apply IFRS 15 over IFRS 9 because IFRS 15 accurately reflects the true nature of Islamic financial transactions. Islamic financial institutions need to transition away from financial instrument-based accounting towards revenue recognition standards that align with trade and asset-backed transactions to preserve authenticity and comply with Shariah principles.

titre: The Decline In China's Trade Share Of GDP: A Structural Accounting
abstract: China’s trade share of GDP has declined since 2007. To understand this, I develop a multi-sector, multi-region Ricardian trade model to quantify the forces driving China’s trade share over 2002–2015. The model incorporates shocks to productivity, international and internal trade costs, labor mobility frictions, and factor supplies, as well as a non-homothetic preference structure. These shocks affect the trade share primarily by reshaping comparative advantage and thereby reallocating sourcing across origins and sectors. Building on the model, I first show that this ratio is pinned down by the domestic expenditure share and is therefore important not only as a measure of openness but also as a reduced-form proxy for gains from trade. I then calibrate the model and conduct a structural decomposition. The results indicate that falling international trade costs, especially in industry, account for most of the rise in China’s trade share from 2002 to 2007. After 2007, fundamental productivity growth, concentrated in industry and services, together with capital deepening, accounts for most of the decline in the trade share, while preference changes induced by non-homothetic preferences play a minor role.

titre: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN EXECUTIVE COMPENSATION, ECONOMIC VALUE ADDED, AND ACCOUNTING PERFORMANCE MEASURES: EVIDENCE FROM CANADIAN LISTED COMPANIES
reference: yes, published in a Journal Journal
abstract: This research paper examines the relation between executive compensation and value-added performance measures Economic Value Added, Market Value Added and accounting performance measures Earnings Per Share, Return on Assets, Return on Equity and Tobin's Q. The results of this empirical analysis demonstrate that there is a significantly relation between executive compensation and Economic Value Added and Market Value Added but a weak and mild relationship with accounting return measures This empirical research is used data of 226 firms which are listed on the Toronto stock exchange for the years 2014-2018 using panel regression methodology.

titre: The Repeal of SAB 121: A Turning Point in Cryptocurrency Regulation and Accounting
abstract: This paper analyzes the recent repeal of SEC's Staff Accounting Bulletin 121, which mandated the classification of client-held crypto assets as liabilities. While criticized for hindering industry participation, its repeal signals a shift towards a more accommodating regulatory framework for digital assets. This shift, driven by factors such as the adoption of Fair Value Accounting and the Trump administration's pro-crypto stance, seeks to support innovation and encourage bank involvement in cryptocurrency custody. However, the repeal also raises concerns about potential risks to investor protection due to reduced regulatory oversight. This paper examines the implications of these changes for the financial industry, assesses the impact of the Trump administration's policies, and explores the path forward for U.S. leadership in digital finance.

titre: AI Agents and Automation in Small Non-Profit Organizations' Accounting Functions
abstract: This paper investigates the potential of AI agents and automation within the accounting functions of small non-profit organizations (NPOs). It explores the benefits of AI, including increased efficiency, reduced errors, and improved decision-making, while also examining the challenges, such as data security, staff training, and ethical considerations. The research analyzes existing literature on AI in accounting, examines the implementation process, reviews available AIpowered accounting software solutions for NPOs, and discusses the critical importance of ethical considerations, including data privacy, algorithmic bias, and the potential for job displacement. The paper concludes by emphasizing the need for a strategic approach to AI adoption, including a focus on scalability, continuous learning, and impact measurement to ensure long-term sustainability and alignment with the NPO's mission.

titre: The Fragility of AI-Driven Strategies: The Bench Accounting Case
abstract: The collapse of Bench Accounting in 2024 offers a cautionary tale about the perils of overreliance on artificial intelligence (AI) in the FinTech industry. While AIdriven solutions promise enhanced efficiency and automation, Bench's rapid demise highlights the dangers of prioritizing technology over fundamental business principles. This paper examines Bench's ambitious AI strategy, financial instability, and failure to align technology with sustainable operations. It explores critical lessons for FinTech startups, emphasizing the importance of human oversight, rigorous testing, and ethical considerations in AI adoption. Drawing from Bench's experience, the paper advocates a balanced approach to AI integration that combines innovation with sound financial management and trustbuilding. This analysis provides actionable insights and lessons learned for FinTech firms navigating the challenges of scaling AI responsibly.

titre: Impact of Environmental Accounting Practices on Shareholder Value in Pharmaceutical Industries: Insights from an Emerging Economy
affiliations: Institute of Management and Technology (IMT), Enugu
abstract: This study examines the impact of environmental accounting practices on shareholder value in pharmaceutical companies in emerging economy from 2010 to 2022. The study specifically investigates the impact of economic, social and environmental performance disclosures on the equity returns in the Nigerian pharmaceutical sector. The analysis focuses on two leading pharmaceutical firms, Neimeth International Pharmaceuticals Plc and Fidson HealthCare Plc, using an ex-post facto research design. Secondary data were sourced from the annual reports and financial statements of these companies. Sophisticated analytical techniques, such as trend analysis and SWOT analysis, coupled with a comparative evaluation of the two firms, were applied to gain valuable insights. Additionally, panel linear regression was conducted to estimate variable coefficients using the E-Views statistical software. The findings reveal that economic and environmental performance disclosures does not have a positive and significant impact on the ROE (with p-values 0.5957 and 0.2415 > 0.05) respectively while social performance disclosure has a negative but statistically significant impact on the ROE (p-value 0.0434 < 0.05). The overall model is statistically significant suggest that the predictors (EPO, SPD, EPD) collectively explain variations in ROE (p-value 0.048607 < 0.05) . It recommends that pharmaceutical firms should enhance economic, social, and environmental disclosures by aligning them with stakeholder expectations and business objectives, adopting cost-effective initiatives, and leveraging integrated reporting to improve transparency, reputation, and financial performance. These findings contribute to a deeper understanding of sustainability accounting's role in aligning organizational goals with stakeholder expectations, particularly in the pharmaceutical sector of emerging economies .

titre: EXPLORING DIGITAL ACCOUNTING PRACTICES IN INDIA
reference: International Journal on Global Business Management and Research (IJGBMR), ISSN 2278 8425, Vol, 12, Issue 2, December 2023
abstract: This study focuses on digital accounting practices in India and how it will be implemented. Now a days all things are done through online like payments, banking activities etc, then the accounting practices are also started done through online or digitally. Digital accounting replace the manual ways of processing financial transactions in almost all organisations. Digital accounting practices are very relevant in the processing of financial transaction. One of the benefits of digital accounting is the improvement in the quality of financial reports. However, this role has often been overlooked. Digital accounting helps to reduces costs, increases revenue and collaboration, improves employee productivity and customer experience at last gain competitive advantages. This says about the Digital accounting practices in India with the benefits of more efficient and competitive, innovative, adapting flexibility and abilities, efficient usage of technologies, through digital accounting the information are transparency and accountability, it will always have the records and it secures the financial information. It also minimises the error and 100% accuracy. Even though, it has some flaws in implementing the digital accounting that are data security, high cost, data backup and recovery, giving training to the employees, complexities and risk of obsolences. The study concluded that digital accounting practices affect the quality of financial reports of firms. The study recommends amongst others, the adaption of digital accounting in all aspects of the processing of financial statements.

titre: Accounting for the Gains from Tariff Reductions
affiliations: Florida International University (FIU) - Department of Economics
abstract: Using the implications of a multi-country, multi-sector framework that are consistent with a class of standard trade models, this paper investigates the gains from tariff reductions for 167 countries. The gains from trade over time are shown to be depending on the weighted average of percentage changes in sectoral tariffs (representing the gains or losses from tariff changes) and relative per capita expenditures across countries, without using any trade elasticity parameters that are subject to potential measurement errors or estimation biases. Over the sample period between 1966 and 2020, the cumulative gains from trade are calculated for each country using sectoral data on domestic and international trade as well as tariffs and relative per capita expenditures, where the gains from tariff reductions are shown to be about 1.8% for the median country, with a range between -4% and 22%. When country-specific results are aggregated, the cumulative global gains from tariff reductions are shown to be about 4.2% (1.3%), when population (gross domestic product) weights are used across countries. The global gains from tariff reductions are shown to be mostly driven by emerging countries, where the sectors of machines, chemicals and vehicles contribute the most in that order. Important policy implications follow as most of the cumulative global gains from trade in emerging countries are shown to be experienced after the Uruguay Round.

titre: Accounting Law and Legal Ownership of Property [Droit comptable et notion juridique de propriété]
reference: Revue de droit public 2024, n°4
abstract: In order to present a true and fair view of the property of public entities, public accounting law relies on the concept of asset, which is distinct from the concept of property. Although the concept of asset does not disregard legal ownership, it often exceeds it or ignores it in favour of economic ownership.

titre: In Search of a Generic Framework of Research on Professions: A view from the Accounting Profession
abstract: This paper aims to build a framework of professions compatible with the accounting profession while consistent with other well-recognised professions. The current problem of studies in professions is to define the phenomenon of profession. Single-profession, taxonomic, and other theory-driven studies have proposed different definitions of a profession. More problematically, many definitions excluded traditional professions such as law, medicine, and medicine. Thus, this paper reviews the current methodology of studies on professions. Also, this paper reviewed the history of accounting practitioners to illustrate the problem of existing paradigms of studies on professions. This paper suggests that an interactionism definition could include well-recognised professions. Based on the definition, this paper proposes a context-neutral, need-based measurement of professionalisation that could be utilised in different social settings.

titre: Divergences Between GLOBE and Accounting Carrying Values
reference: Reprinted from Tax Notes International, Volume 115, No. 12, Pp. 1797-1802
abstract: On June 17 the OECD published its fourth administrative guidance on the model rules of pillar 2, a 144-page booklet. Together with the rules, the commentary, as well as other documents, the organization has produced more than 1,000 pages on pillar 2 since December 20, 2021. This recent administrative guidance refers 31 times to the words “simplify,” “simplified,” and “simplification.” Never mind the fact that most jurisdictions do not have English or French as their official language or that their citizens are also largely not fluent in English as a second language (Brazil being just one of many examples). Also, as I and others have stated in the past, judicial courts across the globe have yet to issue their opinions on the adherence of pillar 2 to their legal systems, and it may be the case that whatever they decide changes its course in the medium to long term. If there was ever a case for handing over corporate tax compliance to artificial intelligence, pillar 2 might be it. In this article, I focus on section 2 of the OECD document. In it, the organization provides guidance on discrepancies that may arise between the “accounting and [global anti-base-erosion] carrying values and the deferred tax assets/liabilities determined therefrom.” Section 2 also contains guidance regarding “the GloBE treatment of an intragroup transaction accounted for at cost by the acquiring Constituent Entity,” and this ties back to guidance released by the OECD earlier this year.

titre: A Study on Impact of National Education Policy 2020 on Accounting Education
abstract: An attempt has been made to study the impact of National education policy 2020 on the accounting education. Researcher had done online survey by using structure questionnaire. The respondents are accounting teachers registered with Ahmedabad branch and suarashtra branch of Indian accounting association. The sample size is 200 samples selected conveniently. Researcher made four type of classification of questionnaire-Impact on Curriculum-on Assessment and Evaluation-Professional development-Student's outcome. Researcher has used nonparametric test-one sample Kolmogorow and Smirnow test to examine the difference in responses. The result of the analysis shows that most of respondents are either agreed or strongly agreed with positive impact of National education policy on Curriculum, assessment and evaluation method, professional development and student's outcome. However in all cases one sample Kolmogorow and Smirnow test indicated significant difference in responses given by respondents.

titre: GO BEYOND GDP: INTRODUCING GROSS OUTPUT, THE "TOP LINE" IN NATIONAL INCOME ACCOUNTING - The Schumpeter Lecture
reference: It is an updated version of the Schumpeter Lecture given in November, 2017, in Stockholm, Sweden.
abstract: Gross output [GO] is the natural measure of the production sector, while net output [GDP] is appropriate as a measure of welfare. Both are required in a complete system of accounts.

titre: A Multiple Case Study Analysis of the Antecedents and Consequences of Robotic Process Automation in Accounting Functions
abstract: Robotic Process Automation (“RPA”) has exploded in popularity since its recent emergence as firms seek to capitalize upon the marketing promise of cost reduction and improved efficiency. Yet, with this expansion of RPA, very little is known about the firms’ initial drivers, perceptions during implementation, and how outcomes align with initial expectations. RPA could displace and dramatically change the workforce, but it is unclear how employees will respond to the continuing spread of RPA. This study seeks to fill that important gap in the scholarly literature by providing insight with a multi-case study approach to better understand the perceptions of technical accounting staff, leadership, and information technology personnel who have directly experienced RPA implementation in their organization.

titre: AI and Web3: A New Era for Accounting and Auditing
abstract: The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence (AI) and Web3 technologies is reshaping the landscape of accounting and auditing. As businesses increasingly adopt these technologies, accounting firms must adapt to meet the evolving needs of their clients. This paper explores the potential benefits and challenges associated with the integration of AI and Web3 into accounting and auditing practices. We delve into specific applications such as fraud detection, automated data entry, and real-time financial analysis. Additionally, we discuss the ethical implications, regulatory considerations, and the necessary skillsets to navigate this evolving technological landscape. By understanding the opportunities and challenges, accounting firms can effectively leverage AI and Web3 to provide innovative and value-added services to their clients.

titre: Bank Accounting Discretion Over the Business Cycle
affiliations: University of British Columbia (UBC) - Sauder School of Business
abstract: This paper documents cross-sectional variation in how banks exercise accounting discretion over the business cycle, and it examines the effects of this behavior on banks’ financial intermediation activities and financial stability. I show that while banks with substantial core deposit bases manage loan loss provisions to smooth earnings over the business cycle, consistent with prior literature, banks with limited core deposits exhibit the opposite behavior. Specifically, they understate provisions during economic upturns and thus overstate them during downturns. This pattern is driven by risk-taking incentives: banks with fewer core deposits reduce provisions in good times to attract wholesale funding, thereby increasing their lending capacity. Additionally, I find that this loan growth enhances these banks’ ability to delay provisions, which in turn facilitates more wholesale funding and lending. This feedback mechanism interacts with the risk-taking channel, functioning as an "accounting accelerator." However, because wholesale funding is information sensitive and loan loss provisions cannot be delayed through bad times, the accounting-driven credit expansion in good times also exacerbates the severity of economic downturns, amplifying business cycle fluctuations and threatening financial stability.

titre: Environmental Accounting and Shareholders' Wealth Creation of Industrial Firms In Nigeria: A Study of Lafarge Nigeria Plc (2013-2022)
affiliations: Institute of Management and Technology (IMT), Enugu
abstract: The study examined how environmental accounting affected shareholders' wealth generation in Nigerian industrial companies during a ten-year period, from 2013 to 2022, with a particular emphasis on Lafarge Nigeria Plc. Evaluating the impact of employee training, corporate social responsibility (CSR), and community development expenses on this company's return on equity (ROE) was one of the main goals. Using secondary data obtained from the company's annual reports and financial statements, an ex-post facto research design was used. Regression analysis was used with e-View version 9 to test the hypotheses. The results showed that the ROE of industrial enterprises in Nigeria is highly impacted by community development expenses (p-value 0.048 < 0.05). However, with p-values of 0.82 and 0.08, respectively, it was shown that there was no significant relationship between staff training expenses and CSR expenditures and ROE. The study came to the conclusion that shareholders' ability to build wealth in Nigerian industrial enterprises is not significantly impacted by environmental accounting. It was recommended that in order to increase value for shareholders and other stakeholders, these companies should improve their environmental accounting procedures.

titre: Environmental Accounting and Shareholders' Wealth Creation of Industrial Firms In Nigeria: A Study of Lafarge Nigeria Plc (2013-2022)
affiliations: Institute of Management and Technology (IMT), Enugu

titre: The True Gender Pay Gap: Accounting for the Distribution of Unpaid Labour
abstract: Women and men’s labour is unequally distributed across market and home production, translating into an uneven allocation of resources across genders. This paper proposes a new approach to measuring gender pay gaps that accounts for both paid and unpaid working hours. Moreover, it extends upon existing decomposition methods to estimate the contribution of segregation across paid and unpaid work to the adjusted gender pay gap. Applying this  method to labour force data in Switzerland, this paper attributes a sizeable and persistent share of the adjusted gender pay gap to segregation across paid and unpaid work,  highlighting the importance of including unpaid home production for understanding earning disparities.  The results stress that  policy makers should focus their attention of the allocation of unpaid work in the economy to further decrease the unequal distribution of resources across genders.

titre: The Future of Accounting: AI-driven Tone Manipulation in SEC Filings
abstract: This paper explores the potential impact of large language model–generated rewritings of 10-K and 10-Q filings. By using LLMs to produce more positively toned versions of these filings, the study compares the sentiment of original versus LLM-rewritten texts using multiple sentiment-analysis techniques, including dictionary-based methods, machine learning models, and LLM-based scores. The results show a notable increase in positive sentiment in the LLM-rewritten filings. Furthermore, among the methods tested, sentiment scores derived from LLMs demonstrate the strongest correlation with stock price movements. A combination of these findings indicates that AI-driven text generation can enhance market perception. These findings carry significant implications for investors and regulators, underscoring the need for careful consideration of AI’s role in financial disclosures.

titre: Bridging the Confidence Gap: Enhancing the Reliability of Self-Reported Accuracy in AI Models for Finance and Accounting Research
reference: The Wharton School Research Paper Jacobs Levy Equity Management Center for Quantitative Financial Research Paper
abstract: This paper examines the reliability of self-reported accuracy in AI models within finance and accounting research and proposes methods to reduce the confidence gap, the discrepancy between a model’s actual accuracy and its self-reported confidence. Researchers in these fields often use self-reported accuracy to construct empirical proxies, yet experiments on financial sentiment analysis reveal a substantial confidence gap of up to 43%. I propose alternative prompting strategies that cut this gap by half and demonstrate that fine-tuned models largely eliminate the issue. Moreover, fine-tuning smaller non-generative models proves to be both resource-efficient and comparably effective to more advanced generative models.

titre: Developing a Framework for Integrating Natural Capital Accounting Data into National Accounting and Budgeting Systems in India
abstract: Natural Capital Accounting (NCA) is a crucial framework that allows for the integration of environmental resources into national accounting systems. In India, rapid economic growth has led to significant environmental degradation, making it imperative to incorporate NCA into national accounts and budgeting systems. Integrating NCA into these systems can support more sustainable policy-making and help India meet its climate goals and Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). This research aims to develop a comprehensive framework for integrating NCA data into India's national accounting and budgeting systems. The focus is on addressing data gaps, institutional challenges, and policy reforms necessary for embedding environmental considerations into India's fiscal planning. A systematic review of secondary data was conducted, including relevant literature, case studies, and policy reports. The study analysed global best practices in NCA integration, examined India's existing national accounting and budgeting systems, and identified key elements required for the successful integration of NCA data. The research highlights that India's national accounting system currently lacks full integration of natural capital, focusing predominantly on economic growth metrics. Global case studies, particularly from countries like the Australia, UK and the Netherlands, offer valuable insights into how India can overcome challenges related to data collection and institutional coordination. A proposed framework is outlined, which includes enhancing data systems, promoting cross-sectoral collaboration, and incorporating NCA data into fiscal policies. Incorporating NCA into India's national accounting and budgeting systems can lead to more sustainable economic policies, ensuring long-term environmental and economic resilience. Policymakers must prioritize data quality, institutional capacity building, and cross-sector collaboration to achieve these goals.

titre: Accounting for the Growth of Real Wages of U.S. Manufacturing Production Workers Since the Nineteenth Century
reference: IZA Discussion Paper No. 17326
affiliations: Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research (SIEPR)
abstract: Why have the real (consumption) wages of U.S. workers risen since the nineteenth century? Some economists answer that increases in real wages have followed increases in labor productivity over time. In this paper, this hypothesized association is confronted with annual observations of changes in the wages and changes in the labor productivity of U.S. manufacturing production workers from the end of the 19th century to the beginning of the 21st century. Correlates with changes in real wages in addition to productivity are considered including statutory legislation, trade unionism, and the state of the business cycle.

titre: Artificial Intelligence Application and Research in Accounting, Finance, Economics, Business, and Management
abstract: Artificial intelligence is a branch of computer science that develop intelligent machines to perform human tasks. Recently, there is growing interest in AI applications in professions that have many processes that can be easily automated. There is widespread optimism that AI systems can lead to new innovations or improve existing processes. This study focuses on some applications of artificial intelligence in the accounting, finance, economics, business, and management profession. The study provides a basic understanding of how AI will be useful in the accounting, finance, economics, business and management professions. The study also offered some insights into the risks posed by the use of artificial intelligence.

titre: Accounting for sustainable livestock production in STIRPAT modeling ecological degradation
abstract: The study examines the contribution of sustainable livestock production to environmental degradation. The aim is to show that exclusion of livestock production activity in modelling environmental degradation using the Stochastic Impacts by Regression on Population, Affluence, and Technology model biases the environmental elasticity of the human population. In particular, it overestimates the environmental impact of human population by approximately 7%. A simple econometric explanation and empirical estimation of the bias in the estimated elasticity of human population is provided in this study. This study is novel in that it is the first empirical study to account for the livestock production activities in the joint ecological impacts of population, affluence and technology. A broader approach in estimating the impacts of the drivers of environmental degradation is therefore recommended.

titre: THE IMPACT OF VOLUNTARY CORPORATE DISCLOSURE ON ACCOUNTING EARNINGS: A STUDY OF INDIAN FIRMS
abstract: Accounting Earnings is the important measurement of any corporate. In the present paper we have studies effect of disclosure of accounting information on Accounting Earnings. We develop a disclosure grid that we use to rate 81 firms listed on the BSE-100 index between 2005 and 2009. We hypothesize that the decision to voluntarily disclose some information is a result of the assessment of the economic costs and benefits to be derived from this additional disclosure. Our results suggest that Indian GAAP firms use depreciation/provisions to smooth earnings. This relation is reduced for firms with high disclosure quality.

titre: Examining the Accounting for the Corporate Bond of the Issuer Subjected to Corporate Reorganization in Line with IAS 27
affiliations: Blantyre International University - Accounting and Finance Department
abstract: Reporting entities preparing financial statements under the IFRSs and IASs always confirm to have complied with all the accounting standards and are required to disclose if there was a diversion from one of them. This study has however found out that a certain corporate bond issuer violated the principles of IAS 27 (2014)-Separate Financial Statements when the new parent company did not adopt the corporate bond of the old parent such that the financial statements of the new parent were substantially different from those of the old parent thereby violating section 13 of IAS 27 (2014) resulting in the material misstatement of the financial statements. There were also no proper disclosures regarding the interest expense on the corporate bonds as such information was absent both on the face of the financial statements as well as the notes. Other pertinent information such as the names of the corporate bond subscribers and any penalty which might have been payable for the early redemption of the bond was not disclosed in the financial statements thereby giving less valuable information to the users of the financial statements. It is recommended that external auditors must advise reporting entities to include all the necessary information in the financial statements for the benefit of the members of the reporting entities as well as other relevant stakeholders. The Malawi Accountants Board should also strengthen its role in the enforcement of Review of Statutory Financial Statements and Financial Reports of Public Interest Entities Regulations 2017.

titre: Accounting Treatment of the Takeover of the Corporate Bond of the Defunct PTC, Wholly Owned Subsidiary -case Study of Press Corporation Plc
reference: Mergers, Acquisitions & Disposals Journal Vol. 4. No. 2 Dec 2023
affiliations: Blantyre International University - Accounting and Finance Department
abstract: There is a general overview that the debts of the subsidiary cannot be settled by the parent company unless the parent had guaranteed them or the courts removed the corporate veil of the subsidiary and concluded with certainty that the subsidiary was in fact just a mere extension or branch of the parent company and therefore required to settle the debts of the subsidiary. The study indicates that Press Corporation Plc took over debts of Peoples Trading Centre (PTC) Limited, its defunct wholly subsidiary, in the form of corporate bonds and loans from the banks which it had guaranteed. MWK7.500 billion corporate bonds were taken over by the parent company and were originally issued to reorganize the loans despite pumping in MWK6.000 billion as equity injection in the PTC. There is a huge possibility that either the purported equity injection did not take place as there were no figures in the separate financial statements of the parent company or alternatively, there was massive concealment of debts of PTC. There is substantial evidence of substantial underreporting of the interest expense on the take over of the PTC Corporate Bonds as only 36 percent of the calculated interest was recognised in the financial statements. The MWK7.500 billion PTC Corporate Bonds were just a delay tactic to write off the debts of the subsidiary as the subsidiary was not supposed to be classified as held for sale in the first place.
